data.title: Twilio soars after 'strong' Q1 sees largely bullish views from analysts
data.description:
Shares of Twilio (TWLO) soared about 20% on Friday after first-quarter results and outlook surpassed estimates, drawing largely bullish reactions from analysts.

Wells Fargo kept its Overweight rating and raised the price target to $200 from $147 on the shares of Twilio — which provides a cloud-based customer engagement platform that enables businesses to send and receive messages, emails, and calls, among other things.

"Results above investor expectations. 1Q results came in far above the buyside bogey while 2Q outlook was in line with what longs were looking for. Both Messaging and Voice accelerated, and TWLO raised its FY outlook across metrics. The Voice AI story appears to be taking shape, which likely gives investors confidence to stick around in the name longer. The setup for the rest of the year also now appears better, even as the comps get more challenging," said analysts led by Ryan MacWilliams.

RBC Capital said it maintained its Underperform rating but increased the price target on the stock to $120 from $100 following first-quarter results on raised estimates.

"Twilio delivered strong Q1 results, sending shares +17% AMC, with revenue (+20% YoY/+16% organic), Non-GAAP operating margin (19.8%), Non-GAAP EPS ($1.50), and FCF ($132.3M) all above consensus. We are pleased with the strong results and continued traction with Voice AI and software add-ons, but remain cautious around competition/long-term margin profile," said analyst Rishi Jaluria and his team.

The analysts noted that Voice AI continues to be a bright spot. Voice revenue accelerated for the sixth straight quarter, growing 20% year-over-year. Twilio's management noted rapid adoption of software add-ons, with Branded Calling and Conversational Intelligence both up more than 100% year-over-year. Non-regulated verticals (e-commerce, food service, retail, etc.) are in the adoptionphase, while more heavily regulated industries like financial services remain hesitant and in experimentation mode, the analysts added.

"Despite strong Q1 results and guidance, we continue to have questions around competition and long-term margins," said Jaluria and his team.

data.title: Cohu forecasts 20% to 25% 2026 revenue growth as it raises HPC outlook to $80M to $100M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cohu (COHU) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “We started the year with strong momentum across multiple product lines with orders up 57% year-over-year,” and “we now see a computing segment opportunity pipeline of approximately $750 million, including roughly $650 million in test handlers and an additional $100 million from HBM inspection,” according to President, CEO & Director Luis Müller.
  • “For fiscal 2026, we’re now increasing our high-performance computing revenue outlook to approximately $80 million to $100 million,” and “we’re emboldened by the opportunity pipeline across 12 customers with 5 customers in qualification stage and another 7 in early engagement stage,” CEO Müller said.
  • “There is a significant SAM opportunity for Cohu in this space and a significant lifetime value in software subscription,” CEO Müller said, adding, “in the first quarter when a $20 million system order came together with $330,000 a year of software subscription, which over the course of the lifetime of these systems could yield approximately $5 million in recurring revenue.”
  • “For Q1 2026, revenue exceeded midpoint of guidance at $125.1 million,” and “gross margin was 46.5%, above guidance,” while “operating expenses were higher than guidance at $55 million, reflecting our decision to scale resources to support the rapid increase in high-performance compute opportunities,” said CFO & Executive Officer Jeffrey Jones.

Outlook

  • “Looking ahead, we expect Q2 revenue to increase 15% sequentially and 34% year-over-year to approximately $144 million, plus or minus $7 million,” CFO Jones said.
  • “We’re increasing our full year 2026 revenue outlook for growth over last year of 20% to 25%,” and “for the full year 2026, we project gross margin in the mid-40% range as we ramp our supply chain and production capacity to support the rapid business expansion in high-performance computing customers,” CFO Jones said.
  • “Operating expenses are expected to be lower than Q1 at about $53 million,” and “we expect quarterly operating expenses through the balance of the year to remain in the low $50 million range,” CFO Jones said.

Financial results

  • “Recurring revenue driven primarily by consumables and typically more stable than systems revenue represented 60% of total revenue,” and “no customer accounted for more than 10% of total sales during the quarter,” CFO Jones said.
  • “Cash and investments increased approximately $5 million during Q1 to $489 million, and cash from operations was $10 million,” while “total debt is $305 million and includes $288 million from the Q4 2025 convertible debt offering,” CFO Jones said.
  • “We’re targeting total capital expenditures to be about 2% of revenue in 2026,” CFO Jones said.

Q&A

  • Brian Chin, Stifel: Asked how much of Q2’s 15% sequential growth is “the ramp in new HPC customer business” vs. “broader base business”; CFO Jones: “the quarter-over-quarter increase in HPC systems revenue was about $10 million,” and “that puts us then for HPC at least systems revenue in the first half of 2026 at roughly about $30 million.”
  • Brian Chin, Stifel: Asked about margins; CFO Jones: “a gross margin split of roughly 50% on recurring, roughly 40% on systems,” and “we think we’re going to end the year somewhere in the mid-40% gross margin.”
  • David Duley, Steelhead Securities: Pressed on AI-driven demand and the “$750 million TAM”; CEO Müller: “we’re not calling it necessarily a TAM... we’re calling it more like a SAM,” and “we have a pretty defined list of customer and customer device classes that we’re tallying up to $750 million.”
  • Craig Ellis, B. Riley: Asked what’s behind the higher HPC revenue outlook and order strength; CEO Müller: “we’re very successful in the qualification of the Eclipse at one particular account,” and on orders, “it’s computing... about 211% year-over-year.”
  • Robert Mertens, TD Cowen: Asked about software monetization; CEO Müller: “we are now currently at an ARR... of about $1.2 million,” and “the attachment rate... is... about 1.3% of our systems have a software subscription attached to it.”
  • Christian Schwab, Craig-Hallum: Asked about M&A; VP of Strategy and Investor Relations Matt Hutton: “we continue to look at opportunities... mostly opportunities in the reoccurring space,” and “a lot of valuations remain elevated, but we’ll continue to be disciplined.”
  • Denis Pyatchanin, Needham: Asked revenue cadence and Q4 seasonality; CFO Jones: “we would expect Q3 to be pretty similar to Q2,” and “Q4... slightly weaker Q4, maybe down Mid-single digit quarter-over-quarter.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and detail-oriented, focusing on the durability of the HPC ramp, margins, and conversion timing (e.g., “Can you maybe give us a sense how much of that is the ramp in new HPC customer business,” Brian Chin, Stifel).
  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and more operationally specific in Q&A, emphasizing pipeline visibility and constraints (e.g., CEO Müller: “we are operating at about a 14 weeks... cycle time,” and CFO Jones: “we are incurring some higher initial costs here to ramp the Eclipse supply chain and production”).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, Q1 2026 language leaned more assertive on compute scale, with CEO Müller shifting from “We closed the first order for a high-performance thermal configuration of the Eclipse handler” (Q4 2025) to “we now see a computing segment opportunity pipeline of approximately $750 million” (Q1 2026).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 introduced a larger, quantified compute opportunity framing (CEO Müller: “opportunity pipeline of approximately $750 million”), while Q4 2025 emphasized cycle recovery and design wins (CEO Müller: “Q4 reflected continued market recovery across end markets”).
  • Q1 2026 guidance language broadened to full-year growth (CFO Jones: “increasing our full year 2026 revenue outlook... 20% to 25%”), versus Q4 2025 focusing on Q1 2026 range (CFO Jones: “guidance for Q1 revenue is approximately $122 million, plus or minus $7 million”).
  • Gross margin discussion shifted from Q4 2025 one-time charges (CFO Jones: “onetime inventory charges”) to Q1 2026 ramp costs (CFO Jones: “higher initial costs here to ramp the Eclipse supply chain and production”).

Risks and concerns

  • Margin and cost pressure tied to scaling Eclipse was a repeated topic (CFO Jones: “we are incurring some higher initial costs here to ramp the Eclipse supply chain and production... through this year”).
  • Lead times and capacity were framed as a gating factor for converting qualifications to revenue (CEO Müller: “lead time really largely depends on the size of the backlog we have in front of it,” and “we are hiring resources in Malaysia... [and] looking at relay out of the facility in Malaysia”).

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a demand inflection led by AI/HPC-driven thermal handling and HBM inspection, highlighted by a “$750 million” targeted compute opportunity set and a higher fiscal 2026 HPC revenue outlook of “$80 million to $100 million.” For Q2, management guided revenue to “approximately $144 million, plus or minus $7 million,” while acknowledging gross margin headwinds from Eclipse ramp costs and committing to continued investment, with operating expenses expected to remain in the “low $50 million” range for the rest of 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street rises as investors look to start May on optimistic note
data.description:
The stock market was higher as Wall Street looked to kick off the month of May on an optimistic note.

S&P 500 (SP500) +0.4%, the Dow (DJI) +0.3%, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +0.4% 

Apple (AAPL) gained 3% in premarket trade after the technology giant reported quarterly results that were above Wall Street’s forecast. Investors were looking ahead to PMI Manufacturing Final and ISM Manufacturing Index data.

U.S. Treasury yields moved higher across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 2.2 basis points to 4.40%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) climbed 2.5 basis points to 3.90%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) advanced 2 basis points to 4.99%.

data.title: Occidental CEO Hollub to retire; Jackson selected as successor
data.description:
Occidental Petroleum (OXY) said Friday its board has named Senior VP and COO Richard Jackson to succeed Vicki Hollub as President and CEO upon her retirement, effective June 1.

Hollub, who has spent a 40-year career at Occidental (OXY) and served as CEO since 2016, when she became the ​first woman to become the ​CEO of a major U.S. oil company, will remain on the board following retirement.

Before her appointment to President and CEO, she served as Occidental's (OXY) President and COO, overseeing the company's oil and gas, chemical, and midstream operations.

Jackson, who joined the company ​in 2003, was appointed as its COO in October 2025; he has held several pivotal leadership positions, including President of U.S. Onshore Oil and Gas, President of Low Carbon Integrated Technologies, General Manager of the Permian Delaware Basin and Enhanced Oil Recovery Oil and Gas, Vice President of Investor Relations, and VP of Drilling Americas.

Hollub's tenure was defined by ​the debt-fueled acquisition of Anadarko Petroleum in 2019, a controversial deal that reshaped Occidental's (OXY) business.

data.title: China's BYD sales fall extends to 8th month as Q1 profit drops 55%: Will EV demand improve?
data.description:
Chinese EV giant BYD (BYDDF) is facing growing pressure as its vehicle sales dropped for the eighth straight month in April, falling 15.5% year-over-year. The slowdown marks BYD’s longest-ever sales decline, driven mainly by weak demand in China’s mass-market EV segment.

Still, overseas business remained a bright spot. The company's passenger vehicle and pickup exports jumped 35% to around 130K vehicles in April, according to Reuters calculations based on a Weibo post by executive Li Yunfei.

The company is increasingly relying on global markets as Chinese buyers shift toward more premium EV models and trade-in subsidies for lower-priced cars lose impact.

To stay competitive, the company is reportedly rolling out EVs with faster-charging batteries and expanding its ultra-fast charging network. At the same time, the company said it will raise prices for its in-house driving-assistant system starting Friday due to higher global memory chip costs.

The pressure is also showing up in earnings. BYD’s Q1 net profit fell 55.4% YoY to ¥4.1B ($599.5M), while revenue dropped 11.8% to ¥150.2B, marking its third consecutive quarterly revenue decline.

Adding to concerns, it has also come under scrutiny in Europe over alleged labor abuses linked to its Hungary operations. Following this market concern, the shares fell 3.3% to ¥102.98 on April 30.

data.title: Estée Lauder delivers a beautiful quarter as turnaround delivers profitable growth, upbeat outlook
data.description:
Estée Lauder’s (EL) successful Beauty Reimagined initiative and merchandise expansion on Amazon (AMZN) and Shopify (SHOP) were reflected in the company’s fiscal third-quarter results, highlighted by increased profitability and margins and free cash flow that more than tripled from a year ago.

The better-than-expected results, coupled with an increase to its full-year outlook, fueled a double-digit gain in Friday’s premarket trading, putting the stock on track to open at its highest level in more than a month.

“Our third-quarter results extend strong year-to-date performance, driven by Beauty Reimagined,” said Stéphane de La Faverie, Estée  Lauder CEO. “Fiscal 2026 is promising to be the pivotal year we intended, one in which we restore organic sales growth and expand our adjusted operating margin for the first time in four years.”

For FQ3, Estée  Lauder (EL) earned an adjusted profit of $0.91, an increase of 40% year-over-year and $0.26 better than expected. With net sales up 5%, the company’s adjusted gross margin widened 140 basis points to 76.4%.

While operating income was negatively impacted by a litigation settlement, excluding this one-time charge, adjusted operating income improved as well, driving the adjusted operating margin to 15%, an improvement of 360 basis points from last year.

By product category, fragrance outperformed with a 12% year-over-year increase in sales, while sales in skin care, makeup, and hair care were roughly flat from a year ago, with declines in its Clinique brand in both skin care and makeup offsetting gains in La Mer, The Ordinary, and the Estée  Lauder makeup brand.

In fragrance, the double-digit gain was driven by the company’s luxury brands, including Le Labo, Kilian Paris, Balmain, and Tom Ford.

On the balance sheet, free cash flow increased to $891M, reflecting strong cash flows from operations and the timing of capital expenditures.

For the remainder of the year, Estée  Lauder (EL) now expects organic new sales growth of ~3% compared to the prior outlook for growth of 1% to 3%, and adjusted operating margin between 10.7% and 11.0% versus 9.8% to 10.2% previously. These estimates consider potential geopolitical disruptions and tariff headwinds.

Additionally, adjusted earnings are now expected to be within the range of $2.35 and $2.45 per share from the prior range of $2.03 and $2.23 per share, with the $2.40 midpoint of the new range exceeding the $2.22 consensus estimate.

data.title: Federal Realty stock up on beat-and-raise Q1, benefited by real estate sale
data.description:
Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) was trading higher after the retail REIT surpassed first-quarter earnings expectations and raised its full-year guidance.

Shares were 1.99% higher at $113.11 during pre-market trading on Friday.

Core FFO of $1.88 for Q1 beats the average analyst estimate by $0.07, and was up from $1.70 recorded in the first quarter of 2025. Revenue of $341.08M (+10.3% Y/Y) beats by $7.95M.

The results included a gain of $92.7M on the sale of real estate, primarily the February sale of Misora at Santana Row. The figure was well above the Q1 2025 figure of $1.2M.

The North Bethesda, Maryland-based company said it signed 101 leases for 649,078 sq. ft. of comparable retail space, a record first-quarter volume.

Commercial portfolio occupancy stood at 93.8% at the end of the first quarter (vs. 93.84% Visible Alpha consensus), up 20 basis points year-over-year. Portfolio leased rate was 96.1%, up 40 basis points year-over-year.

The residential leased rate for comparable properties was 95.6% as of March 31, up 100 basis points year-over-year.

"Our portfolio is performing well amid a volatile macro environment, reflecting both the strength of our platform and the resilience of the higher-income consumer we serve," said CEO Donald Wood.

"With this performance, we are increasing our outlook for 2026, reinforcing our confidence in the consistency and durability of our earnings growth," added Wood.

Core FFO per diluted share guidance for 2026 was raised to a range of $7.46 to $7.55 from $7.42 to $7.52, which exceeds the consensus of $7.48 at the midpoint.

data.title: Carter’s names Sharon Price John as CEO, replacing Douglas Palladini
data.description:
• Carter's Inc (CRI) announced that Sharon Price John has been appointed as the chief executive officer and president, effective June 15, 2026, and will join the board.
  • Richard F. Westenberger will serve as interim CEO and president alongside his CFO/COO role until the transition.
  • John joins from Build-A-Bear Workshop, where she led 13 years of growth, profitability improvement, and business transformation.
  • Her prior experience includes leadership roles at Stride Rite, Hasbro, and Mattel. Douglas C. Palladini has departed as CEO, President, and board member effective immediately.
  • The company reaffirmed its Q1 and full-year fiscal 2026 outlook and will report earnings on May 6, 2026.
